fact_texts: Benvenuto Cellini described himself as a murderer, braggart, insolent, sensual, proud, and passionate.
Benvenuto Cellini was a sculptor, goldsmith, musician, and master of narrative.
Benvenuto Cellini worked in gold and silver, chasing and modeling precious surfaces.
Benvenuto Cellini wrote that he had many things of merit and had a great reputation.
Benvenuto Cellini fought in the Sack of Rome.
In 1565, Cellini married Piera de Salvadore Parigi, a servant who had nursed him when he was sick.
Benvenuto Cellini knew and adored Michelangelo.

Benvenuto Cellini was born in Florence in 1500, son of architect and musician Giovanni, son of Andrea, son of Cristofano; mother Madonna Elisabetta, daughter of Stefano Granacci; father owned a house in Via Chiara and property near Ponte a Rifredi; both parents were citizens of Florence

Cellini alludes to cartoons made by Michelangelo and Leonardo for the Sala del Gran Consiglio in the Palazzo Vecchio at Florence; only shadows remain, with a part of Michelangelo's engraved by Schiavonetti and a transcript by Rubens of Leonardo's called the Battle of the Standard.
Benvenuto Cellini wrote that all men who have done anything of merit should write their life with their own hand.
Benvenuto Cellini's autobiography mentions that after 1565 he showed more tenderness to his children, sister, and nieces.
Benvenuto Cellini's autobiography mentions the Reformation and Counter‑Reformation, the strife of Pope and Emperor and King, but only glimpses.
Benvenuto Cellini's autobiography describes the artistic life of the time, the age of Michelangelo, and his own role among great artists.
Benvenuto Cellini's autobiography describes the social life of the sixteenth century: violence, licentiousness, zeal for fine craftsmanship, vitality, versatility, and idealism.
Benvenuto Cellini fought in the Sack of Rome.
Benvenuto Cellini wrote that all men who have done anything of merit should write their life with their own hand.

In 1565, Cellini married Piera de Salvadore Parigi, a servant who had nursed him when he was sick.

Benvenuto's brother Cecchino, after his first lesson in the school of Giovannino de' Medici, sought clothes from Benvenuto's sisters, who gave him a cloak and doublet that Benvenuto had bought from his savings, leading Benvenuto to discover he had been cheated of his clothes and money.

During his one-year stay in Pisa, Benvenuto studied antiquities such as marble sarcophagi at Campo Santo, made several fine gold and silver works, and abandoned music, particularly the flute, which he hated.
Benvenuto Cellini lived from 1500 to 1571.

After confronting his father, Benvenuto left for Lucca and then Pisa, arriving at age about sixteen, where he met Maestro Ulivieri della Chiostra, who welcomed him into his shop, praised his appearance, and taught him gold, silver, and gem work.

In Rome, Torrigiani apprenticed under Il Firenzuola, Giovanni from Firenzuola in Lombardy, a craftsman in large vases and big plate, and showed him a model for a clasp he had made in Florence at Salimbene's, which pleased the master.

Piero Torrigiani and Buonarroti used to go into the Church of the Carmine to learn drawing from the chapel of Masaccio; Buonarroti would banter all who were drawing there, and Torrigiani once gave him a blow on the nose that broke bone and cartilage.
